def: A decorative cloth laid over the back of an armchair to protect the upholstery from hair oils; named after Macassar oil, a Victorian hair preparation.
The horizontal rail at the front of a chair seat, between the front legs.
The curved or rolled front of an armchair's arm; a defining design feature.
A piece of furniture sold at auction; often the source of restoration commissions.
A small square of calico (about 50 mm) on the underside of a deep-buttoned panel, against which the button twine is anchored.
A type of decorative stitchwork, sometimes used in traditional upholstery cushions.
A hardwood commonly used for traditional chair frames; strong, takes glue well, and accepts tacks.
Fabric cut at 45° to the warp and weft grain; essential for welt strips that wrap around curves.
A horizontal stitch passed through the side face of a stuffed pad without exiting the top, drawing the stuffing toward the rim.
A long cylindrical cushion, typically used at the head of a daybed or the side of a sofa.
Black cloth tacked across the underside of a finished chair to hide the structural work. (US: dust cover / cambric.)
Loops of twine across the hessian, under which loose stuffing is tucked.
A flat-bottomed, dome-shaped wooden foot, common on Chesterfields.
The decorative-and-structural technique of compressing the upholstery at marked points with cloth-covered buttons.
Plain, unbleached cotton cloth used as an under-cover before the show fabric is fitted. (US: muslin.)
A trial fitting of the cover in cheap calico before committing to expensive show fabric.
A light, closely-woven cotton or linen, sometimes used as a dust cover under cushions.
Woven rattan, sometimes used as a seat or back panel in lighter chairs.
A deep-buttoned sofa with rolled arms equal in height to the back.
A cone-shaped wire spring used in traditional springing.
Coconut-husk fibre, sometimes used as a stuffing material in budget work.
Decorative trim, similar to gimp but rope-like rather than woven flat.
A loose, fluffy cotton wadding used between calico and top fabric.
The decorative top edge of a chair back, often with carved show-wood.
The top horizontal rail of a chair back.
A loose padded element, removable and sometimes reversible.
Polyester wadding, used for soft surfaces and cushion wraps; a brand name, now generic.
Buttoning where the buttons compress the pad significantly (25–40 mm dimples). (US: diamond tufting.)
The arrangement of buttons in offset rows, creating a diamond pattern of pleats.
A seat pad that lifts out of a wooden frame's rebate. (US: slip seat.)
A round upholstered stool with turned legs.
A light cloth (cambric or similar) under cushions, to keep dust from the springs.
A general term for an upholstered armchair; the modern descendant of the seventeenth-century “easy chair” with stuffed seat and back.
Stitches forming the firm edge of a stuffed pad; see blind stitch and top stitch.
British upholstery c. 1901–1910; light woods, refined proportions, often loose covers.
A mid-twentieth-century British furniture maker; characteristic light-wood frames, often with elastic webbing.
The direction of the warp threads in a woven fabric; matters for cutting and pattern matching.
Compressed wool or cotton fibre, used as a smoothing layer between calico and top fabric.
The structural layer of stuffing, formed into a rim by stitched edges.
Lighter buttoning where the buttons don't compress the pad significantly.
Polyurethane upholstery foam, sold by density (kg/m³) and thickness.
A small upholstered stool intended for resting the feet; see also pouffe.
A seam where the raw edge is enclosed inside the seam itself; a premium loose-cover finish.
The horizontal rail at the front of a chair seat.
Wire thickness (a lower number means thicker wire); 9-gauge is standard for seat coil springs.
British upholstery c. 1714–1830; restrained design, often with show-wood and gimp trim.
A decorative woven braid covering the join between fabric and show-wood.
Animal-protein adhesive, used for traditional joinery on chair frames.
Modern wood adhesive, suitable for non-heritage frame repair.
A tack hammer with a magnetic face for picking up tacks one-handed.
Coarse jute cloth used as the spring-cover layer; 12 oz weight is standard. (US: burlap.)
Animal-hair stuffing; the gold-standard traditional material.
A specific wing-back design from Howard & Sons, late Victorian.
The fabric panel facing inward toward the sitter on an armchair's arm.
The fabric panel facing the sitter on the chair's back.
An obsolete term for sewing-machine feed; not relevant to modern upholstery.
A natural plant fibre used for webbing, hessian, and twine.
Tree-fibre stuffing, sometimes used in cushions; less common today.
A specific settee design with hinged sides, originally from Knole House in Kent.
The knot used to secure laid cord around spring tops in eight-knot lashing.
An adjustable knot used during buttoning, locked off with a square knot afterwards.
Twisted hemp cord used for spring lashing. (US: spring twine.)
Tying spring heads together with laid cord in the eight-knot pattern.
Animal hide used as a show cover; sold by the hide (about 5 m²).
A natural fabric sometimes used for loose covers and upholstery.
A removable, washable cover that slips over an upholstered piece. (US: slipcover.)
See Hammer, magnetic.
Strong waxed twine used for stitching, bridling, and buttoning.
Medium-density fibreboard; used in modern factory frames, and not appropriate for heritage repair.
A traditional woodworking joint used in heritage chair frames.
A heavy pile fabric, often wool, used in traditional and transport upholstery.
A brass-headed nail used as a visible trim along leather and Chesterfield edges.
A tiny brass nail used to fix gimp braid where glue is inappropriate.
A trade name for zigzag (sinuous) springs.
Synthetic webbing; used in modern outdoor furniture but not in heritage work.
A low upholstered seat or footstool, often with a hinged top for storage.
The fabric panel on the outward-facing side of an armchair's arm.
The fabric panel on the back of the chair, fitted last to cover all other panel edges.
A generic term for stuffing; usage varies between traditional and modern shops.
Aligning a fabric's pattern repeat across panel boundaries.
See Welt. (US: welt / welting.)
A fold of fabric, usually creating a clean line at a corner or between buttons.
Engineered wood; used in modern factory frames and as headboard backings.
A soft, low upholstered seat without legs, often deep-buttoned. (US: pouf / hassock.)
The 1822 patented coil spring that created modern upholstery.
A horizontal structural member of a chair frame.
See Webbing strainer.
A rectangular notch cut into a chair's seat rails to receive a drop-in pad.
A long, needle-like tool used to ease stuffing into shape under scrim.
A wide-bladed chisel used to lift tacks during stripping.
A defined, slightly raised edge along the front of a stuffed seat; see stitched edge.
An armchair arm with a curved or scrolled front, characteristic of Chesterfields.
Light hessian, used to cover the first stuffing before the edges are stitched.
The horizontal rail at the seat level of a chair frame; receives the webbing.
The softer top layer of stuffing applied over the scrim-covered first stuffing.
The visible top cover; the fabric the customer sees.
Visible polished or carved wood on a chair, where the upholstery doesn't extend.
See Zigzag spring.
A long pin used to hold the cover in place temporarily before tacking. (US: upholstery pin.)
The American term for a loose cover.
A stamped-steel U-bracket that fixes zigzag springs to a rail.
The compressed working height of lashed coil springs.
A loose seat cushion; an archaic term, occasionally used.
A pneumatic or manual tool that drives staples; standard for modern construction.
The vertical members of a chair back frame.
The structurally-stitched front rim of a traditional first stuffing.
A generic term for the material that fills upholstery; usually horsehair, foam, or coir.
A chair where the upholstery wraps over the seat rails, rather than sitting in a rebate.
A small (6–8 mm) upholstery tack with a small head, used where the head will be visible.
A standard 13 mm upholstery tack, used for most structural tacking.
See Hammer, magnetic.
A rail to which the upholstery is tacked; usually a seat or back rail.
See Webbing.
See Show fabric.
A through-stitch along the top edge of a stuffed pad, locking the rim shape.
A wholesaler selling materials to the upholstery trade at lower prices than retail.
Decorative finishing work — gimp, welt, nails, and so on.
Buttoned with regular spacing; the buttons may sit on a separate tufted sub-panel.
See Mattress twine.
The bottom face of a chair, covered with a bottoming cloth.
A long, curved needle used for stitching pads and edges.
The craft of fitting padding, springs, and cover to a chair frame.
A V-shaped notch cut into a cover panel to fit around a back stile.
A piled fabric with a characteristic light-reflection direction; needs careful pile-direction handling.
British upholstery c. 1837–1901; deep buttoning, heavy materials, many design variants.
Soft fibrous material between calico and show fabric; cotton felt or Dacron. (US: batting.)
An industrial sewing machine used for upholstery; the foot moves with the fabric.
Strong woven strap (jute, in 50 mm width) used as the bottom support of a seat.
A bat-shaped tool for tensioning webbing strands during fitting. (US: webbing stretcher.)
A fabric-wrapped cord sewn into a seam for reinforcement and decoration.
The vertical or sloped panel rising from the arm-back junction on a wing-back.
An armchair with vertical wings rising to nearly back-height.
The London livery company that has governed the upholstery trade since 1465.
A sinuous wire spring used in modern factory upholstery.
A modern fastening for cushion covers; standard YKK #5 or equivalent.
